edges.explanation: 57.8% unrepresented with massive seat-vote distortions proves FPTP does not produce representative outcomes
The most disproportional result in history with four parties over 10% proves the system cannot translate voter preferences
Massive distortion demonstrates FPTP produces artificial majorities unconnected to public preference
PR projection showing Labour needing to negotiate with Lib Dems or Greens proves coalition dynamics would shift policy toward the evidence base
PR requiring coalition negotiation replaces the adversarial populism FPTP incentivises
Cross-national research consistently finding PR produces better democratic outcomes directly contradicts the FPTP superiority assumption
Comparative evidence that PR produces median-aligned policy supports the claim it would shift UK outcomes toward evidence
Comparative evidence directly justifies the switch to STV
49% vs 26% with FPTP at all-time low proves growing majority support for reform
Public already voting multi-party while FPTP support collapses proves the system has fallen behind voter behaviour
Second-largest chamber in the world staffed by PM patronage proves there is no democratic justification for appointment
The scale and patronage basis of the Lords directly justifies replacement with a smaller elected chamber
Scotland and Austria evidence of higher turnout and civic engagement directly justifies votes at 16
Franchise extension improving engagement supports the claim that structural democratic reform has upstream effects on all policy
NZ maintaining or improving government quality after switching from FPTP to MMP is the most directly relevant precedent
NZ achieving more centrist evidence-based outcomes after adopting PR proves the policy shift is real not theoretical
FPTP not producing better governance supports the structural failure claim
PR changing policy outcomes supports the claim that FPTP structurally causes policy failure across domains
PR shifting policy toward evidence directly supports the upstream intervention claim
Growing public support alongside multi-party voting proves reform is democratically legitimate and practically overdue
No democratic justification for appointment directly supports election of the second chamber
Replacing FPTP depends on the structural failure claim that it produces dishonest policy
Donation caps and state funding depend on the claim that the current system enables undue influence
Elected Lords depends on the finding that appointment has no democratic justification
Franchise extension depends on the upstream intervention claim that broader participation improves policy
Maintaining FPTP directly contradicts the evidence that PR produces better democratic and policy outcomes
Supporting Lords as constituted contradicts the evidence that appointment has no democratic justification
Maintaining voter ID and 18 voting age contradicts evidence from Scotland and Austria supporting votes at 16
Votes at 16 and addressing voter ID inconsistencies match the franchise extension policy
No commitment to PR despite conference vote contradicts the evidence base and represents the structural trap of FPTP beneficiaries resisting reform
Hereditary peer removal and consultation on Lords reform are necessary but insufficient steps toward election
STV for Westminster and local government directly matches the evidence-based electoral system policy
Elected Lords directly matches the evidence-based second chamber policy
Votes at 16, automatic registration, and donation caps match the franchise and standards policies
PR for all elections matches and exceeds the STV for Westminster policy
Elected second chamber directly matches
State funding of parties directly matches the standards policy
PR referendum directionally supports reform though a Citizens' Assembly is more evidence-aligned than a referendum
Smaller elected chamber directly matches the evidence-based Lords replacement policy
PR makes donation caps and state funding more feasible because multi-party politics reduces dependence on large donors
Commons reform creates political momentum for Lords reform as part of a comprehensive constitutional settlement
The public already voting multi-party while FPTP collapses in legitimacy directly supports replacing it with STV

cross_domain_edges.explanation: FPTP incentivising dishonesty is the structural cause of the fiscal conspiracy of silence where parties maintain tax locks despite the evidence
Swing seat politics under FPTP drives immigration dishonesty where targets are set for marginal voters not evidence
Electoral reform as an upstream intervention would change the political incentives that produce NHS underfunding through fiscal dishonesty
PR would give Greens balance of power making environmental and energy policy stronger and more evidence-aligned
PR eliminating safe seats reduces NIMBY dynamics that block housing development in constituencies where MPs face no electoral consequence
